([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *República de Tejas*), or simply **Texas**, was a short-lived sovereign country in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America")[[4]](#cite_note-4) from March 2, 1836, to February 19, 1846. Texas was bordered by [Mexico](/wiki/Centralist_Republic_of_Mexico "Centralist Republic of Mexico") to the west and southwest, the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico") to the southeast, the two U.S. states of [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana") and [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas") to the east and northeast, and [U.S. unorganized territory](/wiki/Territorial_evolution_of_the_United_States "Territorial evolution of the United States") encompassing parts of the current U.S. states of [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma"), [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"), [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), and [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming") to the north. The [Texas Revolution](/wiki/Texas_Revolution "Texas Revolution") began when hostilities broke out on October 2, 1835, shortly before the regime of Mexican President and General [Antonio López de Santa Anna](/wiki/Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna "Antonio López de Santa Anna") adopted a new Mexican constitution known as the [Siete Leyes](/wiki/Siete_Leyes "Siete Leyes") that abolished the authority of the states under the [federal republic](/wiki/First_Mexican_Republic "First Mexican Republic") and established a centralized government. The revolution lasted for over six months. On March 2, 1836, delegates in convention proclaimed the [Texas Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Texas_Declaration_of_Independence "Texas Declaration of Independence"). Major fighting ended on April 21, 1836, with the [Treaties of Velasco](/wiki/Treaties_of_Velasco "Treaties of Velasco") that ended the fighting and secured Texas' independence.

The **Republic of Texas** ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *República de Tejas*), or simply **Texas**, was a short-lived sovereign country in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America")[[4]](#cite_note-4) from March 2, 1836, to February 19, 1846. Texas was bordered by [Mexico](/wiki/Centralist_Republic_of_Mexico "Centralist Republic of Mexico") to the west and southwest, the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico") to the southeast, the two U.S. states of [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana") and [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas") to the east and northeast, and [U.S. unorganized territory](/wiki/Territorial_evolution_of_the_United_States "Territorial evolution of the United States") encompassing parts of the current U.S. states of [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma"), [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"), [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), and [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming") to the north. The [Texas Revolution](/wiki/Texas_Revolution "Texas Revolution") began when hostilities broke out on October 2, 1835, shortly before the regime of Mexican President and General [Antonio López de Santa Anna](/wiki/Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna "Antonio López de Santa Anna") adopted a new Mexican constitution known as the [Siete Leyes](/wiki/Siete_Leyes "Siete Leyes") that abolished the authority of the states under the [federal republic](/wiki/First_Mexican_Republic "First Mexican Republic") and established a centralized government. The revolution lasted for over six months. On March 2, 1836, delegates in convention proclaimed the [Texas Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Texas_Declaration_of_Independence "Texas Declaration of Independence"). Major fighting ended on April 21, 1836, with the [Treaties of Velasco](/wiki/Treaties_of_Velasco "Treaties of Velasco") that ended the fighting and secured Texas' independence.

The Mexican Congress refused to recognize the independence of the Republic of Texas, as the Treaties of Velasco were signed by Santa Anna under [duress](/wiki/Duress "Duress") as prisoner. The majority of the Mexican Congress did not approve the agreement. Much of its territory was controlled and disputed by Mexico or the [Comancheria](/wiki/Comancheria "Comancheria"); Mexico considered it a [rebellious province](/wiki/Secession "Secession") during its entire existence. The [Anglo-American](/wiki/Anglo "Anglo") residents of the area and of the republic were referred to as [Texians](/wiki/Texians "Texians"), while Texans of Mexican descent were referred to as [Tejanos](/wiki/Tejanos "Tejanos").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Having gained independence following the revolution, Texas engaged in complex relations with various countries. European powers ([France](/wiki/July_Monarchy "July Monarchy") and [Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland")), along with the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), hesitated to recognize the new republic, in deference to established relations with Mexico. Over time, they eventually recognized Texas and adopted trade relations. Intermittent conflicts between Mexico and Texas continued into the 1840s.[[6]](#cite_note-henderson121-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[Texas was annexed](/wiki/Texas_annexation "Texas annexation") by the United States on December 29, 1845,[[8]](#cite_note-8) and was admitted to the Union as the [28th state](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_date_of_admission_to_the_Union "List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union") on that day, with the transfer of power from the Republic to the new state of [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") formally taking place on February 19, 1846.[[9]](#cite_note-Himmel1999-9) However, the United States inherited the southern and western border disputes with Mexico, which had refused to recognize Texas' sovereignty or to accept U.S. offers to purchase the territory. Consequently, the annexation led to the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Republic_of_Texas&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Spanish Texas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Republic_of_Texas&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Spanish Texas")]

Main article: [Spanish Texas](/wiki/Spanish_Texas "Spanish Texas")

During the [late Spanish colonial era](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain"), Texas had been one of the *[Provincias Internas](/wiki/Provincias_Internas "Provincias Internas")*, and the region is known in the [historiography](/wiki/Historiography "Historiography") as [Spanish Texas](/wiki/Spanish_Texas "Spanish Texas"). Though claimed by Spain, it was not formally colonized by the empire until competing [French interests at Fort St. Louis](/wiki/French_colonization_of_Texas "French colonization of Texas") were a catalyst for Spain to establish permanent settlements in the area.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The region was long occupied and claimed by the existing indigenous groups of Native Americans. During the period from the 1690s–1710s, sporadic missionary expeditions took place before the Spanish established [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio") as a permanent civilian settlement.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Spanish colonization in Texas was a slow process. Nevertheless, Spain tried to establish missions and presidios to spread Catholicism. They encountered challenges, however, because these lands were heavily populated with indigenous people/populations. Despite this, the Spanish influence has left a long-lasting mark on Texas, shaping its cultural landscape and laying the foundation for future settlements.

Following defeats by the British in North America and Europe, in 1762 Bourbon France ceded to Bourbon Spain most of its claims to the interior of North America, including its claim to Texas, as well as the vast interior west of the Mississippi River, which became [Spanish Louisiana](/wiki/Spanish_Louisiana "Spanish Louisiana").[[12]](#cite_note-12) During the years 1799 to 1803, the height of the [Napoleonic Empire](/wiki/Napoleonic_Empire "Napoleonic Empire") in France, Spain returned Louisiana to France.

Following the loss of numerous troops and failure to suppress the revolution by slaves and free people of color in Saint-Domingue, Napoleon decided to abandon North America; he sold what became known as the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") to the United States. The status of Texas during these transfers was unclear and was not resolved until 1819, when the [Adams–Onís Treaty](/wiki/Adams%E2%80%93On%C3%ADs_Treaty "Adams–Onís Treaty") between Spain and the United States ceded [Spanish Florida](/wiki/Spanish_Florida "Spanish Florida") to the United States, and established a clear boundary between Texas and Louisiana.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Starting in 1810 with the outbreak of the [Mexican War of Independence](/wiki/Mexican_War_of_Independence "Mexican War of Independence"), New Spain sought a different relationship with the Spanish crown. Some Anglo Americans fought on the side of Mexico against Spain in [filibustering](/wiki/Filibuster_(military) "Filibuster (military)") expeditions. One of these, the [Gutiérrez–Magee Expedition](/wiki/Guti%C3%A9rrez%E2%80%93Magee_Expedition "Gutiérrez–Magee Expedition") (also known as the Republican Army of the North), consisted of a group of about 130 Anglo Americans under the leadership of [Bernardo Gutiérrez de Lara](/wiki/Bernardo_Guti%C3%A9rrez_de_Lara "Bernardo Gutiérrez de Lara"). Gutiérrez de Lara initiated Mexico's secession from Spain with efforts contributed by [Augustus Magee](/wiki/Augustus_Magee "Augustus Magee"). Bolstered by new recruits, and led by [Samuel Kemper](/wiki/Samuel_Kemper "Samuel Kemper") (who succeeded Magee after his death in battle in 1813), the expedition gained a series of victories against soldiers led by the Spanish governor, [Manuel María de Salcedo](/wiki/Manuel_Mar%C3%ADa_de_Salcedo "Manuel María de Salcedo").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Spanish_Missions_in_Texas.JPG/250px-Spanish_Missions_in_Texas.JPG)](/wiki/File:Spanish_Missions_in_Texas.JPG)

Spanish missions in Texas

Their victory at the [Battle of Rosillo Creek](/wiki/Battle_of_Rosillo_Creek "Battle of Rosillo Creek") convinced Salcedo to surrender on April 1, 1813; he was executed two days later. On April 6, 1813, the victorious Republican Army of the North drafted a constitution and declared the independent Republic of Texas, with Gutiérrez as its president.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Soon disillusioned with the Mexican leadership, the Anglo Americans under Kemper returned to the United States.

The ephemeral Republic of Texas came to an end on August 18, 1813, with the [Battle of Medina](/wiki/Battle_of_Medina "Battle of Medina"), where the Spanish Army crushed the Republican Army of the North. The harsh reprisals against the Texas rebels created a deep distrust of the Royal Spanish authorities. Veterans of the Battle of Medina became leaders of the [Texas Revolution](/wiki/Texas_Revolution "Texas Revolution") and signatories of the [Texas Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Texas_Declaration_of_Independence "Texas Declaration of Independence") from Mexico 20 years later.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After the failure of the expedition, there was no serious push for a "Republic of Texas" for another six years, until 1819. That year Virginian filibuster [James Long](/wiki/James_Long_(filibuster) "James Long (filibuster)") invaded Spanish Texas in an attempt to liberate the region. The Americans were eager to gain territory where they could extend slavery for agricultural and other development.

[Eli Harris](/w/index.php?title=Eli_Harris&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eli Harris (page does not exist)") led 120 men across the Sabine River to Nacogdoches. Long followed two weeks later with an additional 75 men. On June 22, the combined force declared a new government, with Long as president and a 21-member Supreme Council. The following day, they issued a declaration of independence, modeled on the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"). The document cited several grievances, including "Spanish rapacity" and "odious tyranny" and promised religious freedom, freedom of the press, and free trade. The council allocated 10 square miles of land to each member of the expedition, and authorized the sale of additional land to raise cash for the fledgling government. Within a month, the expedition had grown to 300 members.

The new government established trading outposts near Anahuac along the [Trinity River](/wiki/Trinity_River_(Texas) "Trinity River (Texas)") and the [Brazos River](/wiki/Brazos_River "Brazos River"). Long's Republic of Texas also established the first English-language newspaper ever published in Texas, named the *Texas Republican*. It operated only for the month of August 1819.

Long contacted [Jean Lafitte](/wiki/Jean_Lafitte "Jean Lafitte"), a French pirate who ran a large smuggling operation on [Galveston Island](/wiki/Galveston_Island "Galveston Island"). His letter suggested that the new government establish an admiralty court at Galveston, and offered to appoint Lafitte governor of Galveston. Unbeknownst to Long, Lafitte was serving as a Spanish spy. While making numerous promises – and excuses – to Long, Lafitte gathered information about the expedition and passed it on to Spanish authorities. By July 16, the Spanish Consul in New Orleans had warned the viceroy in Mexico City that "I am fully persuaded that the present is the most serious expedition that has threatened the Kingdom".

Lafitte failed to assist the expedition, which soon ran low on provisions. Long dispersed his men to forage for food. Discipline began to break down, and many men, including [James Bowie](/wiki/James_Bowie "James Bowie"), returned home. In early October, Lafitte reached an agreement with Long to make Galveston an official port for the new country and name Lafitte as governor. Within weeks, 500 Spanish troops arrived in Texas and marched on Nacogdoches. Long and his men withdrew. Over 40 of his men were captured. Long escaped to [Natchitoches, Louisiana](/wiki/Natchitoches,_Louisiana "Natchitoches, Louisiana"). Others fled to Galveston and settled along Bolivar Peninsula.

Undeterred in defeat, Long returned again in 1820. He joined the refugees at Bolivar Peninsula on April 6, 1820, leading more reinforcements. He continued to raise money to equip a second expedition. Fifty men attempted to join him from the United States, but they were arrested by American authorities as they tried to cross into Texas. The men who had joined Long were disappointed they were paid in scrip, and they gradually began to desert. By December 1820, Long commanded only 50 men.

With the aid of Ben Milam and others, Long revitalized the Supreme Council. He later broke with Milam. The expedition led an uncertain existence until September 19, 1821, when Long and 52 men marched inland to capture Presidio La Bahía. The town fell easily on October 4, but four days later Long was forced to surrender by Spanish troops. He was taken prisoner and sent to Mexico City; about six months later he was shot and killed by a guard, who reportedly was bribed to do so by [José Félix Trespalacios](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_F%C3%A9lix_Trespalacios "José Félix Trespalacios"). The Long Expeditions were ended.

### Mexican Texas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Republic_of_Texas&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mexican Texas")]

Main article: [Mexican Texas](/wiki/Mexican_Texas "Mexican Texas")

These colonies were limited in quotas and to specific locations.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Since Mexican independence had been ratified by Spain shortly thereafter, Austin later traveled to Mexico City to secure the support of the new country for his right to colonize.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The establishment of [Mexican Texas](/wiki/Mexican_Texas "Mexican Texas") coincided with the Austin-led colonization, leading to animosity between Mexican authorities and the acceleration of American immigration to Texas. The [First Mexican Empire](/wiki/First_Mexican_Empire "First Mexican Empire") was short-lived, being replaced by a republican form of government in 1823. In 1824, the sparsely populated territories of Texas and [Coahuila](/wiki/Coahuila "Coahuila") were joined to form the state of [Coahuila y Tejas](/wiki/Coahuila_y_Tejas "Coahuila y Tejas"). The capital was controversially located in southern Coahuila, the part farthest from Texas.

Mexico's independence from Spain in 1821, had Texas become part of the newly formed Mexican state. During this period, Stephen F. Austin led a group of American settlers, known as the Old Three Hundred, who were granted permission to establish colonies in Texas. However, tensions between the Mexican government and these American settlers grew, fueled by cultural differences, economic disparities, and the issue of slavery. The Mexican government's attempts to restrict American immigration and enforce its laws led to increased resentment among the settlers, culminating in the Texas Revolution.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Following Austin's lead, additional groups of immigrants, known as [Empresarios](/wiki/Empresario "Empresario"), continued to colonize Mexican Texas from the United States. A spike in the price of cotton, and the success of plantations in Mississippi, encouraged large numbers of white Americans to migrate to Texas and obtain slaves to try to replicate the plantation business model.[[18]](#cite_note-seeds-18) In the [Law of April 6, 1830](/wiki/Law_of_April_6,_1830 "Law of April 6, 1830"), Mexican President [Anastasio Bustamante](/wiki/Anastasio_Bustamante "Anastasio Bustamante") outlawed American immigration to Texas, following several conflicts with the Empresarios over the status of slavery, which had been abolished in Mexico in 1829, but which the Texians refused to end.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Texians replaced slavery with long-term [indentured servitude](/wiki/Indentured_servitude "Indentured servitude") contracts signed by "liberated" slaves in the United States to work around the abolition of slavery. Angered at the interference of the Mexican government, the Empresarios held the [Convention of 1832](/wiki/Convention_of_1832 "Convention of 1832"), which was the first formal step in what became the Texas Revolution.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Texas Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Republic_of_Texas&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Texas Revolution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Bradford_Texas_1838_%28Boston%29_UTA.jpg/250px-Bradford_Texas_1838_%28Boston%29_UTA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bradford_Texas_1838_(Boston)_UTA.jpg)

Map of the Republic of Texas by [Thomas Gamaliel Bradford](/wiki/Thomas_Gamaliel_Bradford "Thomas Gamaliel Bradford"), 1838

Main article: [Texas Revolution](/wiki/Texas_Revolution "Texas Revolution")

By 1834, American immigrants in the area greatly outnumbered Mexicans.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Following a [series of minor skirmishes](/wiki/Anahuac_disturbances "Anahuac disturbances") between Mexican authorities and the immigrants, the Mexican government increased its military presence in Texas throughout 1834 and early 1835. Mexico created a new constitution formalized under President Antonio Santa Anna, called [Siete Leyes](/wiki/Siete_Leyes "Siete Leyes"), and began to strengthen the central government. American immigrants described it as restoring pre-enlightened forms of governance, depriving them of their rights and liberties. They objected to mandatory Catholicism and, when Santa Anna began enforcing anti-slavery laws, many feared religious coercion could be next.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In 1835, the central government split [Coahuila y Tejas](/wiki/Coahuila_y_Tejas "Coahuila y Tejas") into two separate departments. The Texian leadership under Austin began to organize its own military, and hostilities broke out on October 2, 1835, at the [Battle of Gonzales](/wiki/Battle_of_Gonzales "Battle of Gonzales"), the first engagement of the [Texas Revolution](/wiki/Texas_Revolution "Texas Revolution").[[23]](#cite_note-23) In November 1835, a provisional government known as the [Consultation](/wiki/Consultation_(Texas) "Consultation (Texas)") was established to oppose the Santa Anna regime (but stopped short of declaring independence from Mexico). On March 1, 1836, the [Convention of 1836](/wiki/Convention_of_1836 "Convention of 1836") came to order, and the next day declared independence from Mexico, establishing the Republic of Texas.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The influences of English, Mexican, and American revolutionary traditions were embedded into the Texas Declaration of Independence.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Independent republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Republic_of_Texas&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Independent republic")]

#### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Republic_of_Texas&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Politics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Lizars_Mexico_%26_Guatimala_1836_UTA_%28detail_of_Texas%29.jpg/250px-Lizars_Mexico_%26_Guatimala_1836_UTA_%28detail_of_Texas%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lizars_Mexico_%26_Guatimala_1836_UTA_(detail_of_Texas).jpg)

Detail of a map showing the Republic of Texas by [William Home Lizars](/wiki/William_Home_Lizars "William Home Lizars"), 1836

In March 1836, [David G. Burnet](/wiki/David_G._Burnet "David G. Burnet") became the [interim president of Texas](/wiki/President_of_Texas "President of Texas"), and appointed Lorenzo de Zavala as vice president at the same time, following a vote where Burnet won among convention delegates.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Burnet declined to run for reelection, thereafter Sam Houston was [elected](/wiki/1836_Republic_of_Texas_presidential_election "1836 Republic of Texas presidential election") as the new President of the Republic of Texas on September 5, 1836, with Burnet resigning.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The second [Congress of the Republic of Texas](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Republic_of_Texas "Congress of the Republic of Texas") convened a month later, in October 1836, at [Columbia (now West Columbia)](/wiki/Columbia,_Texas "Columbia, Texas"). [Stephen F. Austin](/wiki/Stephen_F._Austin "Stephen F. Austin"), known as the *Father of Texas*, died December 27, 1836, after serving two months as [Secretary of State](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_of_Texas "Secretary of State of Texas") for the new Republic.

In 1836, five sites served as temporary capitals of Texas ([Washington-on-the-Brazos](/wiki/Washington-on-the-Brazos,_Texas "Washington-on-the-Brazos, Texas"), [Harrisburg](/wiki/Harrisburg,_Houston "Harrisburg, Houston"), [Galveston](/wiki/Galveston,_Texas "Galveston, Texas"), [Velasco](/wiki/Velasco,_Texas "Velasco, Texas") and [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia,_Texas "Columbia, Texas")), before [President](/wiki/President_of_the_Republic_of_Texas "President of the Republic of Texas") [Sam Houston](/wiki/Sam_Houston "Sam Houston") moved the capital to [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston") in 1837. The next president, [Mirabeau B. Lamar](/wiki/Mirabeau_B._Lamar "Mirabeau B. Lamar"), moved the capital to the new town of [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas") in 1839.

The first flag of the republic was the "[Burnet Flag](/wiki/Burnet_Flag "Burnet Flag")" (a single gold star on an azure field), followed in 1839 by official adoption of the Lone Star Flag.

Internal politics of the Republic were focused on two factions. The nationalist faction, led by Lamar, advocated the continued independence of Texas, the expulsion of the [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), and the expansion of Texas to the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"). Their opponents, led by Houston, advocated the annexation of Texas to the United States and peaceful coexistence with the Native Americans where possible. The [Congress of the Republic of Texas](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Republic_of_Texas "Congress of the Republic of Texas") even passed a resolution over Houston's [veto](/wiki/Veto "Veto"), claiming [The Californias](/wiki/The_Californias "The Californias") for Texas.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The [1844 Republic of Texas presidential election](/wiki/1844_Republic_of_Texas_presidential_election "1844 Republic of Texas presidential election") split the electorate dramatically, with the newer western regions of the Republic preferring the nationalist candidate [Edward Burleson](/wiki/Edward_Burleson "Edward Burleson"), while the cotton country, particularly east of the [Trinity River](/wiki/Trinity_River_(Texas) "Trinity River (Texas)"), went for [Anson Jones](/wiki/Anson_Jones "Anson Jones").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

#### Armed conflicts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Republic_of_Texas&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Armed conflicts")]

Main articles: [Comanche Wars](/wiki/Comanche_Wars "Comanche Wars") and [Texas–Indian wars](/wiki/Texas%E2%80%93Indian_wars "Texas–Indian wars")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Map_of_the_Republic_of_Texas_and_the_Adjacent_Territories%2C_1841.jpg/250px-Map_of_the_Republic_of_Texas_and_the_Adjacent_Territories%2C_1841.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Republic_of_Texas_and_the_Adjacent_Territories,_1841.jpg)

Map of the Republic of Texas and the Adjacent Territories by [C.F. Cheffins](/wiki/Charles_Cheffins "Charles Cheffins"), 1841

The [Comanche](/wiki/Comanche "Comanche") Indians, whose territory included northwest Texas,[[30]](#cite_note-30) furnished the main tribal opposition to the Republic, manifested in multiple raids on Mexican, indigenous, and European settlements.[[31]](#cite_note-31) In the late 1830s, Sam Houston negotiated peace between Texas and the Comanches. When Lamar ascended to presidency in 1838 he reversed the Houston administration's policy towards the Native Americans. War soon resumed with the Comanches and Lamar ordered an invasion of [Comancheria](/wiki/Comancheria "Comancheria") itself. In retaliation, the Comanches attacked Texas in a series of raids. After [peace talks](/wiki/Council_House_Fight "Council House Fight") in 1840 ended with the massacre of 34 Comanche leaders in [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio"), the Comanches launched a major attack deep into Texas, known as the [Great Raid of 1840](/wiki/Great_Raid_of_1840 "Great Raid of 1840"). Under command of Potsanaquahip ([Buffalo Hump](/wiki/Buffalo_Hump "Buffalo Hump")), 500 to 700 Comanche cavalry warriors swept down the [Guadalupe River](/wiki/Guadalupe_River_(Texas) "Guadalupe River (Texas)") valley, killing and plundering all the way to the shore of the Gulf of Mexico, where they sacked the towns of [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Texas "Victoria, Texas") and [Linnville](/wiki/Linnville,_Calhoun_County,_Texas "Linnville, Calhoun County, Texas"). The Comanches retreated after being pursued by 186 rangers and were caught at the [Battle of Plum Creek](/wiki/Battle_of_Plum_Creek "Battle of Plum Creek"), in which they lost the plunder they had taken.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Houston became president again in 1841 and, with both Texians and Comanches exhausted by war, a new peace was established.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The newly-founded Republic of Texas had ambitions to increase its territory, including the annexation of eastern New Mexico, and to control and plunder the trade along the [Santa Fe Trail](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Trail "Santa Fe Trail") from the United States to New Mexico. In 1841, to those ends, President Lamar unofficially supported the [Santa Fe Expedition](/wiki/Texan_Santa_Fe_Expedition "Texan Santa Fe Expedition"). Texas merchants accompanied by 320 soldiers invaded New Mexico. The expedition failed; the participants were captured and imprisoned, although eventually released to return to Texas.[[34]](#cite_note-McClure-34) In 1843, Texans, with the support of their government, conducted [two large raids](/wiki/Texas_raids_on_New_Mexico_(1843) "Texas raids on New Mexico (1843)") on New Mexico and commercial traffic on the Santa Fe Trail. Both raids failed. The raiders killed several Mexican citizens and soldiers, but were defeated by New Mexican [ciboleros](/wiki/Cibolero "Cibolero") to end the first raid and forced to surrender to the U.S. army on the second.[[35]](#cite_note-Myers-35)

On its part, Mexico refused to recognize Texas's independence.[[36]](#cite_note-36) On March 5, 1842, a Mexican force of over 500 men, led by [Ráfael Vásquez](/wiki/R%C3%A1fael_V%C3%A1squez_(Mexican_general) "Ráfael Vásquez (Mexican general)"), invaded Texas for the first time since the revolution. They soon headed back to the Rio Grande after briefly occupying [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio"). About 1,400 Mexican troops, led by the French mercenary general [Adrián Woll](/wiki/Adri%C3%A1n_Woll "Adrián Woll"), launched a second attack and captured San Antonio on September 11, 1842. A Texas militia retaliated at the [Battle of Salado Creek](/wiki/Battle_of_Salado_Creek_(1842) "Battle of Salado Creek (1842)") while simultaneously, a mile and a half away, Mexican soldiers [massacred](/wiki/Dawson_Massacre "Dawson Massacre") a militia of fifty-three Texas volunteers who had surrendered after a skirmish.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) That night, upon their defeat, the Mexican Army retreated from San Antonio, back to Mexico, while being pursued by Texas soldiers. The Texas Army took the city of Laredo, at which point they were given official orders from [Sam Houston](/wiki/Sam_Houston "Sam Houston") to return to San Antonio. Several soldiers ignored this order, and launched a failed battle to retake [Ciudad Mier](/wiki/Ciudad_Mier "Ciudad Mier").[[39]](#cite_note-39)

The [Naval Battle of Campeche](/wiki/Naval_Battle_of_Campeche "Naval Battle of Campeche") took place in two engagements on April 30, 1843, and May 16, 1843. The battle featured the most advanced warships of its day, including the [Mexican](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") [steamer](/wiki/Steamship "Steamship") *[Guadalupe](/wiki/ARM_Guadaloupe "ARM Guadaloupe")* and the equally formidable *[Montezuma](/wiki/ARM_Montezuma "ARM Montezuma")* which engaged a squadron of vessels from the [Republic of Yucatan](/wiki/Republic_of_Yucatan "Republic of Yucatan") and the Republic of Texas. The latter force consisted of the Texas Navy flagship sloop-of-war *[Austin](/wiki/Texan_sloop-of-war_Austin "Texan sloop-of-war Austin")*, commanded by [Commodore](/wiki/Commodore_(rank) "Commodore (rank)") [Edwin Ward Moore](/wiki/Edwin_Ward_Moore "Edwin Ward Moore"), the [brig](/wiki/Brig "Brig") *[Wharton](/wiki/Texan_brig_Wharton "Texan brig Wharton")*, and several [schooners](/wiki/Schooner "Schooner") and five [gunboats](/wiki/Gunboat "Gunboat") from the Republic of Yucatán, commanded by former [Texas Navy](/wiki/Texas_Navy "Texas Navy") [Captain](/wiki/Captain_(naval) "Captain (naval)") James D. Boylan. The battles were inconclusive.[[40]](#cite_note-40) A scene from this battle is engraved on the cylinder of every [Colt 1851 Navy](/wiki/Colt_1851_Navy_Revolver "Colt 1851 Navy Revolver"), 1860 Army, and [1861 Navy](/wiki/Colt_1861_Navy_Revolver "Colt 1861 Navy Revolver") revolver.

The Republic of Texas's relationship with Native American tribes was marked by persistent conflict. Tribes such as the Comanche and the Cherokee occupied much of the territory claimed by the Republic, leading to significant controversy. Although some treaties were signed to establish peace, the terms were frequently violated, resulting in ongoing violence. The Republic struggled to maintain a strong military presence to defend its borders against these tribes due to limited resources. To resolve these tensions, Tehuacana Creek Councils, with the absence of the Comanches, led to a treaty. The negotiated treaty is known as the [Treaty of Tehuacana Creek](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tehuacana_Creek "Treaty of Tehuacana Creek"), which was signed in 1844. The agreement led to the effective dismantilation of the Tehuacana Creek Council, which included tribes such as, the Cherokees, Wacos, Caddos, Anadarkos, Hainas, Delawares, Shawnees, Tawakonis,Lipan Apaches, and southern Comanches.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Cabinet_Card_of_Comanche_Chief_Tosawa_by_W._S._Soule%2C_edit.jpg/250px-Cabinet_Card_of_Comanche_Chief_Tosawa_by_W._S._Soule%2C_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cabinet_Card_of_Comanche_Chief_Tosawa_by_W._S._Soule,_edit.jpg)

[Chief Tosahwi](/wiki/Tosahwi "Tosahwi") of the [Penateka Comanche](/wiki/Comanche#Meusebach–Comanche_treaty "Comanche")

The Treaty of Tehuacana Creek, while significant in its attempt to establish lasting peace, faced considerable challenges in implementation. The diverse tribal groups involved held varying levels of commitment to the agreement, and the Republic of Texas lacked the resources and manpower to consistently enforce its terms. Furthermore, ongoing disputes over land and resources, coupled with the continued encroachment of settlers, fueled tensions and undermined the treaty's effectiveness. The fragile peace established by the treaty ultimately proved short-lived, as conflicts between the Republic and various [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") tribes persisted throughout the remainder of the Republic's existence.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Conflicts between Texas and Mexico intensified conflicts between political factions, including an incident known as the [Texas Archive War](/wiki/Texas_Archive_War "Texas Archive War"). To "protect" the Texas national archives, President Sam Houston ordered them removed from Austin. The archives were eventually returned to Austin, albeit at gunpoint. The Texas Congress admonished Houston for the incident, and this episode in Texas history solidified Austin as Texas's seat of government for the Republic and the future state.[[43]](#cite_note-archivewar-43)

There were also domestic disturbances. The [Regulator–Moderator War](/wiki/Regulator%E2%80%93Moderator_War "Regulator–Moderator War") involved a land feud in Harrison and Shelby Counties in [East Texas](/wiki/East_Texas "East Texas") from 1839 to 1844. The feud eventually involved [Nacogdoches](/wiki/Nacogdoches,_Texas "Nacogdoches, Texas"), San Augustine, and other East Texas counties. Harrison County Sheriff [John J. Kennedy](/wiki/John_J._Kennedy_(Republic_of_Texas_politician) "John J. Kennedy (Republic of Texas politician)") and county judge Joseph U. Fields helped end the conflict, siding with the law-and-order party. Sam Houston ordered 500 militia to help end the feud.

## Borders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Republic_of_Texas&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Borders")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Political_divisions_of_Mexico_1836-1845_%28location_map_scheme%29.svg/250px-Political_divisions_of_Mexico_1836-1845_%28location_map_scheme%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Political_divisions_of_Mexico_1836-1845_(location_map_scheme).svg)

Map of region, 1835–1846

Territory which declared its independence from Mexico, 1835–1846

Territory claimed by the Republic of Texas

Territory claimed by the [Republic of the Rio Grande](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Rio_Grande "Republic of the Rio Grande")

Other rebellions

The Republic of Texas claimed borders based upon the Treaties of Velasco between the newly created Republic of Texas and General [Antonio López de Santa Anna](/wiki/Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna "Antonio López de Santa Anna"). The eastern boundary had been defined by the [Adams–Onís Treaty](/wiki/Adams%E2%80%93On%C3%ADs_Treaty "Adams–Onís Treaty") of 1819 between the United States and the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), which recognized the [Sabine River](/wiki/Sabine_River_(Texas%E2%80%93Louisiana) "Sabine River (Texas–Louisiana)") as the eastern boundary of [Spanish Texas](/wiki/Spanish_Texas "Spanish Texas") and western boundary of the [Missouri Territory](/wiki/Missouri_Territory "Missouri Territory").

Under that treaty, the United States had renounced its claim to Spanish land to the east of the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains") and to the north of the [Rio Grande](/wiki/Rio_Grande "Rio Grande"), which it claimed to have acquired as part of the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") of 1803.

The Texian leaders at first intended to extend their national boundaries to the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"), but ultimately decided to claim the [Rio Grande](/wiki/Rio_Grande "Rio Grande") as boundary, including much of [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), which the Republic never controlled. They also hoped, after peace was made with Mexico, to run a railroad to the [Gulf of California](/wiki/Gulf_of_California "Gulf of California") to give "access to the East Indian, Peruvian and Chilean trade".[[44]](#cite_note-44) When negotiating for the possibility of annexation to the U.S. in late 1836, the Texian government instructed its minister Wharton in Washington that if the boundary were an issue, Texas was willing to settle for a boundary at the [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_divide "Drainage divide") between the [Nueces River](/wiki/Nueces_River "Nueces River") and Rio Grande, and leave out New Mexico.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

The southern and western boundary of the Republic of Texas with Mexico was disputed throughout the republic's existence, since Mexico refused to acknowledge the independence of Texas. Texas claimed the [Rio Grande](/wiki/Rio_Grande "Rio Grande") as its southern boundary, while Mexico insisted that the [Nueces River](/wiki/Nueces_River "Nueces River") was the boundary. In practice, much of the disputed territory was [occupied by the Comanche people](/wiki/Comancheria "Comancheria") and outside the control of either state. Texian claims included the eastern portions of [New Mexico](/wiki/Santa_Fe_de_Nuevo_M%C3%A9xico#Mexican_territory "Santa Fe de Nuevo México"), which was administered by Mexico throughout this period. Some contested regions (such as modern Texas southwest of the [Pecos River](/wiki/Pecos_River "Pecos River")) remained essentially self-governing while sending representatives to both the Mexican and Texian Congresses.

## Criteria of citizenship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Republic_of_Texas&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Criteria of citizenship")]

Citizenship was not automatically granted to all previous inhabitants of Texas, and some residents were not allowed to continue living legally within the Republic without the consent of Congress. The Constitution of the Republic of Texas (1836) established different rights according to the race and ethnicity of each individual. Section 10 of the General Provisions of the Constitution stated that all persons who resided in Texas on the day of the Declaration of Independence were considered citizens of the Republic, excepting "Africans, the descendants of Africans, and Indians."[[46]](#cite_note-46) For white immigrants, Section 6 established to become citizens, they needed to reside in Texas for at least six months and take an oath of loyalty to the Republic; in contrast, under Section 9, African slaves brought to Texas were to remain slaves and could not be emancipated without the consent of Congress. Furthermore, the Congress was not allowed to make laws banning or restricting either slavery or the slave trade. Section 9 also established that: "No free person of African descent, either in whole or in part, shall be permitted to reside permanently in the Republic, without the consent of Congress."[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The Republic of Texas experienced significant immigration from both the United States and Europe. Settlers from the southern states brought their cultural and social norms, which included slavery. Slavery became a "cornerstone" of the Texian economy particularly in the agricultural sector. The institution of slavery had fueled the economy but also was able to reinforce social inequality. While slavery was officially abolished in Mexico in 1829, many Texians continued to practice it. This led to tensions with the Mexican government.[[48]](#cite_note-Willingham_466–493-48)

Women in the Republic of Texas, while often marginalized, still played crucial roles in society. They contributed to the economy through domestic labor, agriculture, and business. However, their legal rights were very limited and they faced significant social and economic obstacles. While some women were able to challenge traditional gender roles, the majority of them were still very confined.[[48]](#cite_note-Willingham_466–493-48)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Republic_of_Texas&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Alamo_1936_Issue-3c.jpg/330px-Alamo_1936_Issue-3c.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alamo_1936_Issue-3c.jpg)

[Sam Houston](/wiki/Sam_Houston "Sam Houston") and [Stephen F. Austin](/wiki/Stephen_F._Austin "Stephen F. Austin") depicted on a 1936 US postage stamp commemorating 100th anniversary of the Texas Republic

In September 1836 Texas elected a [Congress](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Republic_of_Texas "Congress of the Republic of Texas") of 14 senators and 29 representatives. The [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Republic_of_Texas "Constitution of the Republic of Texas") allowed the first [president](/wiki/President_of_the_Republic_of_Texas "President of the Republic of Texas") to serve for two years and subsequent presidents for three years. To hold an office or vote, a man had to be a citizen of the Republic.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

The first Congress of the Republic of Texas convened in October 1836 at [Columbia](/wiki/West_Columbia,_Texas "West Columbia, Texas") (now West Columbia). [Stephen F. Austin](/wiki/Stephen_F._Austin "Stephen F. Austin"), often referred to as the "Father of Texas", died on December 27, 1836, after serving just two months as the republic's secretary of state. Due mainly to the ongoing war for independence, five sites served as temporary capitals of Texas in 1836: ([Washington-on-the-Brazos](/wiki/Washington-on-the-Brazos,_Texas "Washington-on-the-Brazos, Texas"), [Harrisburg](/wiki/Harrisburg,_Texas "Harrisburg, Texas"), [Galveston](/wiki/Galveston,_Texas "Galveston, Texas"), [Velasco](/wiki/Velasco,_Texas "Velasco, Texas") and Columbia). The capital was moved to the new city of [Houston](/wiki/Houston,_Texas "Houston, Texas") in 1837.

In 1839, a small pioneer settlement situated on the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River_(Texas) "Colorado River (Texas)") in central Texas was chosen as the republic's seventh and final capital. Incorporated under the name Waterloo, the town was renamed [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas") shortly thereafter in honor of Stephen F. Austin.

Unlike the [constitution of the United States](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States") which provided for federalism, Texas had instead established a centralized [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state").[[a]](#cite_note-53)

The Republic of Texas established a robust legal system, including a Supreme Court to interpret and uphold the Constitution. The Constitution itself could be amended through a two-thirds vote in both houses of Congress, followed by ratification by the people. Major constitutional changes during this period often related to issues of land grants, taxation, and the powers of the executive and legislative branches.[[53]](#cite_note-54) The court system inaugurated by Congress included a [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_Republic_of_Texas "Supreme Court of the Republic of Texas") consisting of a chief justice appointed by the president and four associate justices, elected by a joint ballot of both houses of Congress for four-year terms and eligible for re-election. The associates also presided over four judicial districts. Houston nominated [James Collinsworth](/wiki/James_Collinsworth "James Collinsworth") to be the first chief justice. The county-court system consisted of a chief justice and two associates, chosen by a majority of the justices of the peace in the county. Each county was also to have a sheriff, a coroner, justices of the peace, and constables to serve two-year terms. Congress formed 23 counties, whose boundaries generally coincided with the existing municipalities. In 1839, Texas became the first nation in the world to enact a [homestead exemption](/wiki/Homestead_exemption "Homestead exemption"), under which creditors cannot seize a person's primary residence.

The Republic's political landscape was marked by factionalism with two primary groups emerging, the War Party and the Peace Party. The War Party, led by figures like Mirabeau B. Lamar, advocated for a more aggressive stance against Native American tribes and Mexico which favoring expansion and military action. The Peace Party, associated with Sam Houston prioritized diplomacy, compromise, and a more cautious approach to foreign policy. These factions often clashed over key issues shaping the Republic's domestic and foreign policies/affairs.[[54]](#cite_note-55) "Prior to this time, factions were the order of the day. Before the Revolution, there was the "Peace Party" and the "War Party". Once the path to Revolution was clear, the "Peace" faction was subsumed by the "War" faction, and for a brief time, Texians seemed to agree with each other, at least in terms of politics" (65).[[55]](#cite_note-56)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Republic_of_Texas&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Baylor_University_1892_front.png/250px-Baylor_University_1892_front.png)](/wiki/File:Baylor_University_1892_front.png)

[Baylor University](/wiki/Baylor_University "Baylor University"), 1892 lithograph

President [Anson Jones](/wiki/Anson_Jones "Anson Jones") signed the charter for [Baylor University](/wiki/Baylor_University "Baylor University") in the fall of 1845.[[56]](#cite_note-57) [Henry Lee Graves](/wiki/Henry_Lee_Graves "Henry Lee Graves") was elected Baylor's first president. It is believed to be the oldest university in Texas; however, [Rutersville College](/wiki/Rutersville_College "Rutersville College") was chartered in 1840 with land and the town of [Rutersville](/wiki/Rutersville,_Texas "Rutersville, Texas").[[57]](#cite_note-58) Chauncey Richardson[[58]](#cite_note-Stone2010-59) was elected Rutersville first president. The college later became [Southwestern University](/wiki/Southwestern_University "Southwestern University") in Georgetown, [Williamson county](/wiki/Williamson_County,_Texas "Williamson County, Texas").[[58]](#cite_note-Stone2010-59) [University of Mary Hardin-Baylor](/wiki/University_of_Mary_Hardin-Baylor "University of Mary Hardin-Baylor") was also chartered by the Republic of Texas in 1845, and received lands in [Belton, Texas](/wiki/Belton,_Texas "Belton, Texas").[[59]](#cite_note-60) Wesleyan College, chartered in 1844 and signed by president [Sam Houston](/wiki/Sam_Houston "Sam Houston"), another predecessor to Southwestern did not survive long due to competition from other colleges.[[60]](#cite_note-61) [Mirabeau Lamar](/wiki/Mirabeau_Lamar "Mirabeau Lamar") signed a charter in 1844 for the Herman University for medicine but classes never started due to lack of funds.[[61]](#cite_note-62) The University of San Augustine was chartered June 5, 1837, but did not open until 1842 when Marcus A. Montrose became president. There were as many as 150 students enrolled. However, attendance declined to 50 in 1845, and further situations including animosity and embittered factions in the community closed the university in 1847.[[62]](#cite_note-63) Later it became the University of East Texas, and soon after that became the Masonic Institute of San Augustine in 1851. [Guadalupe College](/wiki/Guadalupe_College "Guadalupe College") at [Gonzales](/wiki/Gonzales,_Texas "Gonzales, Texas") was approved January 30, 1841; however, no construction efforts ensued for the next eleven years.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

## Diplomatic relations and foreign trade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Republic_of_Texas&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Diplomatic relations and foreign trade")]

Main article: [Foreign relations of the Republic of Texas](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_the_Republic_of_Texas "Foreign relations of the Republic of Texas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/H%C3%B4tel_Bataille_de_Franc%C3%A8s.jpg/250px-H%C3%B4tel_Bataille_de_Franc%C3%A8s.jpg)](/wiki/File:H%C3%B4tel_Bataille_de_Franc%C3%A8s.jpg)

The Hôtel Bataille de Francès (now [Hôtel de Vendôme](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_de_Vend%C3%B4me_(place_Vend%C3%B4me,_Paris) "Hôtel de Vendôme (place Vendôme, Paris)")), [Place Vendôme](/wiki/Place_Vend%C3%B4me "Place Vendôme") in Paris, housed the Embassy of the Republic of Texas.

During its existence, the Republic of Texas received diplomatic recognition from only six nations: [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), the [Republic of Yucatán](/wiki/Republic_of_Yucat%C3%A1n "Republic of Yucatán"), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"), and the [United States of America](/wiki/United_States "United States"). Texas' status as a slaveholding country and Mexico's claim on the territory caused significant problems in the foreign relations of Texas with other nations.[[18]](#cite_note-seeds-18)

Despite this, these nations adopted trade relations with Texas, and in turn, provided for friendly relations with them. Its main exports were predominantly crops, such as cotton and corn. Raw materials like iron, as well as machinery and other consumer goods like tea, beer, and chocolate were traded as well. Outside of these countries, Texas did export some goods to [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), and in some instances, to [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") and [Spain](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), though amounts were minimal.[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66)

Although it was supported by the vast majority of Texians at the time of independence,[[66]](#cite_note-67) annexation by the United States was prevented by the leadership of both major U.S. political parties, the [Democrats](/wiki/History_of_the_Democratic_Party_(United_States) "History of the Democratic Party (United States)") and the [Whigs](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_States) "Whig Party (United States)"), who opposed the introduction of a vast slave-holding region into a country already divided into pro- and anti-slavery sections and wished to avoid a war with Mexico.

On March 3, 1837, U.S. President [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson") appointed [Alcée La Branche](/wiki/Alc%C3%A9e_Louis_la_Branche "Alcée Louis la Branche") as *[chargé d'affaires](/wiki/List_of_ambassadors_of_the_United_States_to_Texas "List of ambassadors of the United States to Texas")* to the Republic of Texas, thus officially recognizing Texas as an independent republic.[[67]](#cite_note-68) France granted official recognition of Texas on September 25, 1839, appointing Alphonse Dubois de Saligny to serve as *chargé d'affaires*. The [French Legation](/wiki/French_Legation,_Texas "French Legation, Texas") was built in 1841, and still stands in Austin as the oldest frame structure in the city.[[68]](#cite_note-69) Conversely, the Republic of Texas embassy in Paris was located in what is now the [Hôtel de Vendôme](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_de_Vend%C3%B4me_(place_Vend%C3%B4me,_Paris) "Hôtel de Vendôme (place Vendôme, Paris)"), adjacent to the [Place Vendôme](/wiki/Place_Vend%C3%B4me "Place Vendôme") in the [1st arrondissement of Paris](/wiki/1st_arrondissement_of_Paris "1st arrondissement of Paris").[[69]](#cite_note-70) The Kingdom of Denmark gave recognition to Texas in turn for free trade. Although no embassy was established, Texas had created a legation in Austin.

The United Kingdom hesitated to grant official recognition to the Republic of Texas due to its own friendly relations with Mexico, but nevertheless admitted Texian goods into British ports. In [London](/wiki/London "London"), opposite the gates to [St. James's Palace](/wiki/St._James%27s_Palace "St. James's Palace"), the original Embassy of the Republic of Texas is now a hat shop but is clearly marked with a large plaque and there was a nearby restaurant in [Trafalgar Square](/wiki/Trafalgar_Square "Trafalgar Square") called the Texas Embassy Cantina, which closed in June 2012.[[70]](#cite_note-71) A plaque on the exterior of 3 St. James's Street in London notes that the upper floors of the building housed the Texas Legation.

The United Kingdom eventually recognized Texas in the 1840s after a cotton price crash, in a failed attempt to coerce Texas to give up slavery (replacing slave-produced cotton from southern U.S. states) and to stop expansion of the United States to the southwest.[[18]](#cite_note-seeds-18) The cotton price crash of the 1840s bankrupted the Republic, increasing the urgency of finding foreign allies who could help prevent a reconquest by Mexico.[[18]](#cite_note-seeds-18)

The Republic of Texas faced significant economic challenges, including the establishment of a stable financial system.While some banks were established, the lack of a centralized banking system and the volatility of the Texas dollar made financial transactions difficult. Private banking played a crucial role, but it was often unregulated and subject to fluctuations in the market.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

## Annexation by the U.S.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Republic_of_Texas&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Annexation by the U.S.")]

Main article: [Texas annexation](/wiki/Texas_annexation "Texas annexation")

On February 28, 1845, the [U.S. Congress](/wiki/Congress_of_the_United_States "Congress of the United States") passed a bill that authorized the United States to annex the Republic of Texas. On March 1, [U.S. President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [John Tyler](/wiki/John_Tyler "John Tyler") signed the bill. The legislation set the date for annexation for December 29 of the same year. Faced with imminent American annexation of Texas, [Charles Elliot](/wiki/Charles_Elliot "Charles Elliot") and Alphonse de Saligny, the British and French ministers to Texas, were dispatched to Mexico City by their governments. Meeting with Mexico's foreign secretary, they signed a "Diplomatic Act" in which Mexico offered to recognize an independent Texas with boundaries determined with French and British mediation. Texas President [Anson Jones](/wiki/Anson_Jones "Anson Jones") forwarded both offers to a specially elected convention meeting at Austin, and the American proposal was accepted with only one dissenting vote. The Mexican proposal was never put to a vote. Following the previous decree of President Jones, the proposal was then put to a vote throughout the republic.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Texas_Statehood_1945_Issue-3c.jpg/250px-Texas_Statehood_1945_Issue-3c.jpg)](/wiki/File:Texas_Statehood_1945_Issue-3c.jpg)

Postage stamp issued on the 100th anniversary of Texas statehood, 1945

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Texas_proposed_boundaries.svg/250px-Texas_proposed_boundaries.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Texas_proposed_boundaries.svg)

Proposals for Texas's north and west boundaries in 1850 debate

On October 13, 1845, a large majority of voters in the republic approved both the American offer and the proposed constitution that specifically endorsed [slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") and emigrants bringing slaves to Texas.[[72]](#cite_note-73) This constitution was later accepted by the U.S. Congress, making Texas a U.S. state on the same day annexation took effect, December 29, 1845 (therefore bypassing a [territorial](/wiki/Territory_of_the_United_States "Territory of the United States") phase).[[73]](#cite_note-74) One of the motivations for annexation was the huge debts which the Republic of Texas government had incurred. As part of the [Compromise of 1850](/wiki/Compromise_of_1850 "Compromise of 1850"), in return for $10,000,000 in Federal bonds, Texas dropped claims to territory that included parts of present-day [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"), [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma"), [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), and [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming").

The resolution included two unique provisions: First, it said up to [four additional states](/wiki/Texas_divisionism "Texas divisionism") could be created from Texas' territory with the consent of the State of Texas and pursuant to the admissions process of the federal constitution. New states north of the [Missouri Compromise Line](/wiki/Missouri_Compromise_Line "Missouri Compromise Line") would be [free states](/wiki/Slave_and_free_states "Slave and free states"), while those south of the line could opt to become [slave states](/wiki/Slave_and_free_states "Slave and free states"). Though the resolution did not make exceptions to the constitution,[[74]](#cite_note-75) the U.S. Constitution does not require Congressional consent to the creation of new states to be *ex post* to applications, nor does the U.S. Constitution require applications to expire.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Second, Texas did not have to surrender its public lands to the federal government. While Texas did cede all territory outside of its current area to the federal government in 1850, it did not cede any public lands within its current boundaries. Consequently, the lands in Texas that the federal government owns are those it subsequently purchased. This also means the state government controls [oil reserves](/wiki/Oil_reserves "Oil reserves"), which it later used to fund the state's public university system through the [Permanent University Fund](/wiki/Permanent_University_Fund "Permanent University Fund").[[75]](#cite_note-76) In addition, the state's control over offshore oil reserves in Texas runs out to 3 [nautical leagues](/wiki/Nautical_league "Nautical league") (9 nautical miles, 10.357 statute miles, 16.668 km) rather than three nautical miles (3.45 statute miles, 5.56 km) as with other states.[[76]](#cite_note-77)[[77]](#cite_note-78)
